hero.title: Locked-price auto transport.
hero.description: Real-time tracking. Photo evidence at every step. A damage protection promise. Built for individual customers who deserve more than a quote and a hope.

triplePromise.title: Three commitments. Built into every shipment.

price.headline: You pick the speed, we lock the price.

damage.headline: Your car is covered beyond the carrier's insurance.

people.headline: You'll always talk to a real person, by name.

hero.title: Price Promise
hero.subtitle: You pick the speed. We lock the price. The quote is the contract.

antiPattern.title: The dirty open secret of auto transport pricing

body.0: Most brokers will quote you a price, take your deposit, then call you back two weeks later with a higher number when they actually go to find a carrier. The industry has a name for this: “the bid-and-bump.”
body.1: It's the reason auto transport reviews online are full of customers feeling burned. The original quote was bait. The real number was always going to be higher — the broker just needed your deposit first.

antiPattern.punchline: We don't do that. The price you book is the price you pay. Period.

tiers.title: Three tiers. Same price-lock guarantee on all three.
tiers.intro: Pricing scales with urgency. Whichever tier you pick, the guarantee is identical: the booking price is the final price.

standby.description: Most flexible. Best value when your timeline can flex.

priority.description: Tight enough to plan around, flexible enough to stay affordable.

expedited.description: When timing matters more than price. We get on it immediately.

commitment.headline: The number on your booking confirmation is the number on your final invoice.

items.4: Any ancillary fee disclosed before you book

0.q: What if a carrier wants more money to take my car?
0.a: That's our problem, not yours. The price you booked is the price you pay. If a carrier wants to charge more, we either find a different carrier or absorb the difference. Your invoice does not change after booking.

1.q: What does the locked price include?
1.a: Door-to-door transport from your origin to destination, standard loading and unloading, basic carrier insurance coverage during transit, real-time tracking access, photo evidence at pickup and delivery, and a named coordinator from quote through delivery. Taxes, where applicable, are included.

2.q: What's NOT included in the price?
2.a: Inoperable vehicle surcharges (winching/heavy equipment), oversized vehicle premiums (lifted trucks, dual axles), enclosed-trailer upgrades, and additional insurance beyond standard cargo coverage. We always flag any add-on cost before you book — never after.

3.q: What if I need to cancel or reschedule?
3.a: Free cancellation up until carrier assignment. After assignment, cancellation may incur a small dispatch fee, which we'll always disclose before applying. Rescheduling within your service tier's window is always free.

4.a: Three factors: distance, vehicle type, and tier urgency (Standby, Priority, or Expedited). We pull live carrier rates from our verified network and add a transparent operations margin. No surge pricing. No bid-against-yourself markups.

5.q: Why don't other brokers do this?
5.a: Most brokers operate on a bid model: they post your shipment to a carrier marketplace and accept whichever bid wins. The original quote is just a guess — when actual costs come in higher, you eat the difference or your shipment gets stuck. We pre-negotiate carrier rates and absorb price variance ourselves. It's a different business model, not a different industry.

cta.title: See your locked price in 60 seconds.
cta.description: Enter your origin, destination, and vehicle. We'll show three tiered prices instantly. Pick one, lock it, done.
cta.secondary: See how the People Promise works →

hero.title: Damage Promise
hero.subtitle: Your car is covered beyond the carrier's insurance. We handle the claim. You don't chase.

antiPattern.title: What happens to most people when their car arrives damaged

body.0: Customer files a claim. The broker says “contact the carrier directly — they're the responsible party.” Customer calls the carrier. Carrier says “file with our insurance.” Insurance asks for photos the customer doesn't have. Customer gets bounced for weeks. Most give up. The few who don't spend three months on hold.
body.1: This pattern happens because most brokers don't want damage claims to be their problem. Legally, the carrier is the responsible party. So the broker steps back.

antiPattern.punchline: We don't step back. We own the claim from the moment you file it. Your only job: send us the photos and tell us what happened.

coverage.title: Layered protection. Carrier coverage + our backstop.

bond.label: Broker bond on file
bond.description: FMCSA-required protection. Backstops carrier coverage gaps.

contingent.label: Contingent cargo liability
contingent.description: Our policy. Kicks in when carrier insurance falls short.

ack.label: Claim acknowledgment SLA
ack.description: From the moment you file, our coordinator confirms receipt and starts the process.

resolution.label: Resolution target
resolution.description: Most claims resolve faster. 14 days is our outer commitment, not our average.

doorDing.title: Door ding during loading
doorDing.detail: Minor body damage during pickup or drop-off. Carrier insurance handles it; we expedite the paperwork.

theft.title: Theft from carrier in transit
theft.detail: Whether items inside the vehicle (within reason) or the vehicle itself. Police report required.

tireDebris.title: Tire/wheel damage from road debris
tireDebris.detail: Open carriers expose vehicles to highway debris. Not covered — though enclosed transport eliminates the risk.

weather.title: Hail or weather damage
weather.detail: Acts of God are excluded across the industry. Enclosed transport is the recommended hedge for weather-sensitive vehicles.

mechanical.title: Mechanical issue mid-route
mechanical.detail: If the carrier's handling caused the damage (e.g., dropped during loading). Not covered if the vehicle had pre-existing issues documented at pickup.

steps.title: Three steps. We do the rest.

1.title: Note damage at delivery
1.detail: Have the driver write the damage on the BOL before they leave. Photo the delivery walkaround.

2.title: Submit through portal
2.detail: In your customer portal: click 'File a claim,' upload photos and the BOL. Takes ~3 minutes.

3.title: We take over
3.detail: Within 24 hours, your coordinator confirms. Within 14 days, claim is resolved. You don't talk to carriers.

0.q: What if my car is damaged in transit?
0.a: File a claim with us within 24 hours of delivery. We acknowledge within 24 hours and target full resolution within 14 days. We handle the carrier directly — you don't chase, you don't mediate. Required: photos at pickup (we provide), photos at delivery, and the BOL signed at delivery noting the damage.

1.q: Who pays for the damage — you or the carrier?
1.a: Carriers carry mandatory cargo insurance (typically $100K–$250K). For most damage events, the carrier's insurance covers the claim. If their coverage falls short, our $500,000 contingent cargo liability and $75,000 broker bond kick in. The customer never sees that complexity — we handle it on the back end.

2.a: Acts of God (hail, wind, flooding), road debris damage on open carriers, pre-existing damage documented at pickup, personal items left in the vehicle, and damage caused by customer-provided faulty loading instructions. For high-value or weather-sensitive vehicles, we recommend our enclosed-trailer upgrade.

3.q: What if the carrier refuses to pay?
3.a: That's where our broker bond and contingent cargo coverage engage. We pursue the carrier on your behalf, but you don't wait for the recovery. Once the claim is approved by us (within 14 days), payout to you happens regardless of carrier-side resolution timing.

4.q: Do you require enclosed shipping for damage protection?
4.a: No. Both open and enclosed shipping are covered under the same Damage Promise. Open carriers carry slightly higher exposure to weather and road debris, which is why we strongly recommend enclosed for vehicles over $75,000, classics/collectibles, low-clearance sports cars, and vehicles with custom paint or wraps.

5.a: At pickup: a 360° walkaround photo set timestamped and geotagged, plus the Bill of Lading (BOL) signed by the driver. At delivery: a matching 360° walkaround set + delivery BOL. Both surface in your customer portal as soon as the driver app captures them. This evidence is the foundation of any claim.

cta.title: Ship with the broker that owns the claim.
cta.description: Locked price. $575K of layered protection. A 14-day claim resolution target. Real coordinators. Get a quote in 60 seconds.

hero.title: People Promise
hero.subtitle: You'll always talk to a real person, by name. Family-owned. Bilingual. Accountable.

antiPattern.title: What faceless brokering actually looks like

body.0: You call a 1-800 number. You get a sales rep. They book your shipment, take your deposit, then hand you off to dispatch. Dispatch hands off to a driver. The driver doesn't speak to you. Something goes wrong, you call back, get a different sales rep, who has to read the file from scratch.
body.1: By the time you've explained your situation three times, your car is somewhere in Texas and nobody on the phone knows where.

antiPattern.punchline: Auto transport got broken when it became faceless. We're rebuilding the operator-customer relationship the way it should have always worked: one named person, one phone number, one point of accountability.

commitments.title: Four things we promise. Backed by how we've operated since 2014.

named.title: Named coordinator on every shipment
named.description: When you book, you're assigned a coordinator by name. They know your shipment. They pick up the phone. From quote through delivery — same person.

bilingual.title: Bilingual front-line
bilingual.description: Native Spanish and English coverage on the customer-facing team. The whole site is translated end-to-end, not just bolt-on widgets.

family.title: Family-owned and operated since 2014
family.description: Real names on the company. Decision authority sits with the people doing the work. We've built this on individual accountability.

noScript.title: No script. No transfer maze.
noScript.description: Your coordinator owns the answer. If something goes wrong, the same person calls you back — not a different sales rep starting from zero.

timeline.title: A typical shipment, end to end

0.description: You get a quote. Pick a tier. Book online. Within an hour, your coordinator emails you their direct line and a quick onboarding rundown.

1.description: Your coordinator finds the carrier, validates their FMCSA standing, and confirms pickup details with the driver. You get a notification with the driver's name + truck details.

2.description: Driver arrives. Walks the vehicle with you. Photos go into your portal in real-time. BOL signed. You and your coordinator both have a copy. Truck rolls.

3.description: GPS tracking and milestone updates surface in your portal. If anything unusual comes up — weather delay, mechanical, route change — your coordinator calls you proactively.

4.description: Driver coordinates window with you. Walks the vehicle on arrival. Photos captured. BOL signed. If there's any damage to flag, you note it on the BOL right there.

5.description: Same coordinator stays your point of contact for any claim, follow-up, or future bookings. We don't recycle you back into the inbound funnel.

0.q: Who is my coordinator and how do I reach them?
0.a: At booking, you're assigned a coordinator by name. You'll get their direct number and email. They own your shipment from booking through delivery. If they're unavailable, a backup coordinator picks up — never an automated tree, never an offshore call center.

1.a: Yes. Native Spanish speakers handle inbound calls and customer-facing communications in Spanish from booking through delivery. The Spanish layer of the website (launching Phase A) is a full translation, not a machine-translated overlay. About one in ten US auto-transport customers prefer Spanish — we built for them on day one.

2.q: What does “family-owned and operated” actually change?
2.a: Decision authority sits with the people doing the work. When something goes wrong, escalation doesn't mean climbing a corporate ladder — it means walking down the hall. We've built the company since 2014 by treating individual reputation as the company's reputation.

3.q: What if my coordinator is on vacation when something goes wrong?
3.a: Every coordinator has a designated backup, briefed on your shipment before any handoff. You don't restart from scratch. The backup knows your origin, destination, vehicle, timeline, and any open items. Your point of accountability stays continuous.

4.q: Do I get the coordinator's direct phone number?
4.a: Yes — at booking. Their direct line, their email, and their working hours. We don't hide behind a general 800 number. The whole point of the People Promise is that there's a real person accountable to you.

5.q: How is this different from other brokers' “dedicated agent” pitches?
5.a: Most “dedicated agents” in this industry are sales reps who hand off to dispatch after booking. You never speak to that original person again. Our coordinators stay with the shipment through delivery — including during transit, at delivery, and for any post-delivery questions or claims.

cta.title: One named person. One phone number. One accountable point of contact.
cta.description: Get a locked price in 60 seconds. Then meet your coordinator — by name, with their direct line.

hero.title: A family-owned auto transport company built for real customers.
hero.description: No call-center scripts, no bait-and-switch quotes, no surprise surcharges at delivery. The way auto transport should have worked from the start.

about.buildStatus: 🚧 Build status: credentials, team bios, and customer reviews on this page show placeholders while we confirm final copy and gather photos. Operationally we've been moving cars for years — the website is the part that's still being built.

story.title: We started Auto Line Logistics because the industry had earned its bad reputation.

body.0: Most auto transport companies in this country are brokers — they take your deposit, post your car to a load board, and hope someone picks it up at the lowest bid. When a carrier finally accepts the load, they raise the price. And if your car shows up damaged? You're on your own with the carrier's insurance.
body.1: We do this differently. We're a family-owned company based in Southern California. We operate our own trucks where the route makes sense, and we contract with a vetted network of partner carriers everywhere else — every one of them held to the same standards.
body.2: The number on your quote is the number on your invoice. Your coordinator is a real person whose name you'll know. And your car is covered above and beyond the carrier's minimums — because cars get damaged, and pretending otherwise is how customers get burned.

family.description: Independent. Not a call-center, not a franchise.

coverage.description: Layered cargo coverage on every shipment, beyond the carrier's policy.

usBased.description: Your coordinator works from our office, not an offshore answering service.

promiseTieIn.description: These aren't marketing lines — each one is enforceable. Read the full breakdown of what we cover and what you're owed if we miss.

antiScam.title: The auto transport playbook to watch out for.
antiScam.lead: If you've gotten a quote that felt too good to be true, it probably was. Here's the pattern most customers run into — and how we structure things differently.

items.0: Lowball quote to win the booking
items.1: Listed on a load board and ignored until a carrier bites
items.2: Price raised once carriers refuse the original rate
items.3: Hand-off at delivery — broker disappears if anything went wrong
items.4: Damage = your problem with the carrier's insurance, not yours

items.0: Locked price at booking — written into the agreement
items.1: Vetted carrier network we've worked with for years, plus our own trucks
items.2: No surcharge clause — if costs change, that's our problem, not yours
items.3: Named coordinator from quote through delivery
items.4: Layered coverage and we file the damage claim if it happens

team.title: Real names. Real phone numbers.
team.description: You don't get routed through a queue. The person who quotes your shipment is the same person who coordinates pickup, monitors transit, and is reachable if something changes mid-route.

ben.name: Ben Baatarjargal
ben.bio: Founded Auto Line Logistics and runs day-to-day operations. Owner-operator background — knows what the trucks see because he's been in them.

coordinator.name: TBD
coordinator.bio: Bio + photo pending. Coordinators are the named contacts on every shipment — the People Promise in practice.

dispatch.name: TBD
dispatch.bio: Bio + photo pending. Manages our owned trucks and the carrier-partner network that handles routes outside our direct fleet.

team.footnote: Photo and bio confirmations pending — Ben to provide. We'd rather show placeholders than stock-photo a team page.

credentials.title: Licensed, bonded, and insured.
credentials.description: Verifiable through the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) and the Better Business Bureau. Our numbers will display below the moment Ben confirms them on the SAFER lookup.

verifyCallout.body: Once our MC and DOT numbers are published, you can pull our full safety and authority record on
verifyCallout.afterLink: . That's how legitimate carriers and brokers get checked. If a company refuses to share its MC and DOT numbers, walk away.

reviews.title: Reviews from real shipments.
reviews.description: We're not posting fabricated testimonials. The reviews section will populate once we've collected verifiable customer quotes — with first names, route, and date — that we can attribute honestly.
reviews.emptyState: Customer reviews coming soon. We'd rather leave this space empty than fill it with fake quotes.
reviews.footnote: Recently shipped with us? Email info@autolinelogistics.com — we'd love to feature your experience.

0.q: Are you a broker or a carrier?
0.a: Both — and we're upfront about it. We operate our own trucks on routes where it makes operational sense, and we contract with a vetted network of partner carriers everywhere else. Every carrier we work with is held to the same standards as our own fleet. The Damage Promise applies regardless of who's driving.

1.a: Southern California. Our office is the operational hub for the corridors we cover most heavily — California to Hawaii, California to Alaska, and cross-country routes. We'll publish the full work address once we update this page.

2.q: How long have you been in business?
2.a: Auto Line Logistics has been moving customer vehicles for years. The exact founding date will post here once Ben confirms it for the record — we don't want to round numbers on a page that ought to be precise.

3.q: Why is the website still being built if you've been operating for years?
3.a: Operationally we've always run on direct relationships — referrals, repeat customers, military families who pass our number around. The website is the new part: a place to lock a price without a phone call, see what we cover, and verify our credentials yourself. Customer coordination has always been the strength; we're catching the website up to it.

4.q: How do I verify you're a legitimate carrier?
4.a: Look us up on FMCSA SAFER. Our MC and DOT numbers will publish here once confirmed. Any auto transport company that won't share those numbers — or whose record on SAFER doesn't match what they tell you on the phone — is one to walk away from.

5.a: Yes. California is our home base, but we ship to every state. The most-requested corridors — Hawaii, Alaska, Texas, Florida, New York — each get their own dedicated page. Don't see your route? Get a quote and your coordinator will lock a price.

hero.title: Should you ship it or drive it?

inputs.zipUnknown: ZIP not in our v1 table — get a real quote

ship.label: Ship with Auto Line Logistics
ship.title: Door-to-door, locked when you book
ship.titleAlaska: Inland to Tacoma, RoRo to Anchorage
ship.invalidTitle: Need a real ZIP to estimate
ship.invalidTimeline: Get a coordinator quote in 1 business hour.
ship.invalidMsg: We pull pricing from a corridor table for v1 — odd or international ZIPs need a human price.

drive.label: Drive it yourself
drive.titleOcean: There's an ocean in the way.
drive.subtitleOcean: Not on the table for Hawaii routes.
drive.oceanMsg: Cars travel by RoRo vessel (Matson or Pasha) from Long Beach or Oakland. We coordinate the inland leg, port handoff, and island delivery as one booking.
drive.invalidMsg: Check the destination ZIP, or use the city-name fallback (v2 upgrade).

verdict.shipWins: Shipping saves you ~${savings} and {hours} hours of driving.
verdict.shipWinsSub: Plus you skip the hotel, the truck stops, and the chance of a rock chip on your windshield somewhere along the way.
verdict.driveWins: For this trip, driving saves you ~${savings}.
verdict.driveWinsSub: Under 200 miles, you'll usually beat the truck — and that's the honest answer. We're here when you need the ship side.
verdict.washSub: Within $50 either way. Pick the experience you want — drive if it's a road-trip story, ship if you'd rather skip the windshield time.
verdict.hawaii: Hawaii means shipping — that's just geography.
verdict.hawaiiSub: We've made the inland-to-ocean handoff as turnkey as possible. You see one price and one timeline.
verdict.alaska: You CAN drive the Alaska Highway. Most customers don't.
verdict.alaskaSub: A week through Canada in bear country, two border crossings, and you usually pay more than the ship route.
verdict.invalid: We couldn't recognize that ZIP — but we can still get you a real price.
verdict.invalidSub: Submit a quote with your destination and a coordinator replies in 1 business hour with a locked price and the drive comparison.

shipVsDrive.footnote: The estimated ship price is our best guess, not your locked price. Real prices clear in 1 business hour after you submit a quote — that's when we run actual carrier pricing. Drive-side estimate uses $150/night hotels, $50/person/day food, $0.20/mi for vehicle wear (real marginal cost, not the IRS depreciation rate). Distance pulled from our top-80 corridor table — Google Maps API integration coming next.

metadata.title: All corridors — Auto Line Logistics
metadata.description: Locked-price auto transport on every major US corridor. California to Hawaii, Alaska, Texas, Florida, New York, and more. Door-to-door, named coordinator, no surprises.

hero.title: Every major US corridor. Same locked-price guarantee.
hero.description: Whether your shipment crosses an ocean or three time zones, the promise is the same: the quote is the contract.
hero.cta: Don't see your route? Get a quote anyway →

live.description: Detailed routing, sub-destinations, and corridor-specific FAQ for each. Click through for the full breakdown.

comingSoon.titleTemplate: {count} more corridors with dedicated pages this build cycle.
comingSoon.description: Already serving these corridors operationally — dedicated corridor pages launching as we build them out. Ship today by getting a quote; the corridor page just makes the process visible.

finalCta.title: Find your corridor.
finalCta.descriptionTemplate: {count} corridors mapped. Hundreds more shippable on request. Your origin ZIP and destination ZIP are all we need to lock a price.

californiaHawaii.shortDesc: Door-to-door to all four major islands. Port logistics, ocean booking, and inland trucking under one locked price.

oahu.name: Oahu — Honolulu
oahu.routing: CA → Long Beach/Oakland port → Honolulu port → your address
oahu.note: Primary mainland-Hawaii port. Most sailings per week, fastest turnaround. Default destination if you ship to Oahu.
oahu.customers: Military PCS to Pearl Harbor / JBPHH, mainland vehicle buyers, college students

maui.name: Maui — Kahului
maui.note: Inter-island barge from Honolulu. Add 2–4 days to the Oahu baseline for the second leg.
maui.customers: Snowbirds, retirees relocating, second-home owners, hospitality workers

bigIsland.name: Big Island — Hilo or Kona
bigIsland.routing: CA → Long Beach/Oakland → Honolulu → Hilo or Kona (inter-island)
bigIsland.note: Two delivery ports. We route to Hilo (east side) or Kona (west side) based on which is closer to your destination address.
bigIsland.customers: Coffee farm owners, ranch operations, retirees, science / observatory staff

kauai.name: Kauai — Lihue
kauai.note: Smallest Hawaiian port. Inter-island barge from Honolulu typically 2–4 days, fewer sailings per week than Maui or Big Island.
kauai.customers: Retirees, vacation-home owners, hospitality industry, healthcare workers

californiaAlaska.shortDesc: Two routes — overland via the Alaska Highway or ocean via Tacoma. We pick the right one for your season and destination.

anchorage.name: Anchorage / JBER
anchorage.routing: CA → Tacoma port → Anchorage (ocean, default) OR overland via the Alaska Highway
anchorage.note: Default destination. Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson (JBER) is the largest military base, primary destination for Air Force / Army PCS. Best year-round route reliability.
anchorage.customers: Military PCS (JBER, Air Force, Army), oil/gas commuters, retirees, healthcare workers

fairbanks.name: Fairbanks / Eielson AFB / Fort Wainwright
fairbanks.routing: CA → Tacoma → Anchorage → Fairbanks (ocean route, +1–2 days inland) OR overland direct
fairbanks.note: Interior Alaska. Eielson AFB and Fort Wainwright are the major military bases here. Overland is often faster in summer due to direct routing.
fairbanks.customers: Military PCS (Air Force at Eielson, Army at Wainwright), university faculty, oil/gas

matSu.name: Mat-Su Valley — Wasilla, Palmer
matSu.routing: CA → Tacoma → Anchorage → Mat-Su (ocean route, +1 day inland)
matSu.note: Anchorage suburbs. Same primary routing as Anchorage with a short final inland leg. Common destination for working-class families and Anchorage commuters.
matSu.customers: Anchorage commuters, retirees, working-class families, oil/gas crews

southeast.name: Juneau / Ketchikan / Sitka
southeast.routing: CA → Tacoma → southeast ports via Alaska Marine Highway (ferry-based)
southeast.note: Southeast Alaska destinations route through the Alaska Marine Highway System. Longer timelines and fewer sailings — your coordinator will confirm specifics at quote time.
southeast.customers: Government workers (state capital is Juneau), tourism industry, retirees, fisheries

californiaTexas.shortDesc: California to Houston, Dallas, Austin, and San Antonio. Open and enclosed transport.

californiaFlorida.shortDesc: Coast-to-coast, most popular snowbird corridor. Heavy enclosed-trailer demand for collector vehicles.

californiaNewYork.shortDesc: Coast-to-coast. NYC metro and upstate destinations. Multi-vehicle relocation packages available.

californiaArizona.shortDesc: Phoenix, Scottsdale, Tucson. Strong snowbird and retiree relocation traffic.

californiaNevada.shortDesc: Las Vegas, Reno, Carson City. Quick turnaround, often same-week dispatch.

californiaWashington.shortDesc: Seattle, Tacoma, Spokane. Pacific Northwest corridor with abundant carrier capacity.

californiaOregon.shortDesc: Portland, Salem, Eugene. I-5 corridor, frequent sailings.

californiaColorado.shortDesc: Denver, Boulder, Colorado Springs. Mountain-state relocations and seasonal moves.

floridaNewYork.shortDesc: The classic snowbird corridor. Heavy seasonal patterns — northbound spring, southbound fall.

texasFlorida.shortDesc: Houston, Dallas → Miami, Tampa, Jacksonville. Cross-Gulf corridor.

metadata.title: Ship a car California to Hawaii — Port logistics, locked pricing
metadata.description: Mainland-to-Hawaii vehicle transport via Long Beach or Oakland ports. Door-to-port-to-door coordination, all islands. Locked-price quotes. Trusted by military PCS families and snowbird relocations.

hero.title: Ship a car to Hawaii, the right way.
hero.description: Door-to-door, mainland to all four major islands. Port logistics, ocean booking, and inland trucking — coordinated under one locked price.

whyDifferent.title: It's not auto transport. It's logistics.

body.0: Most mainland auto-transport corridors are one truck, one driver, one straight shot. Hawaii is different. Your vehicle has to clear two truck legs, an ocean voyage, port paperwork at both ends, and (for outer islands) an inter-island barge. Six handoffs. Six chances for something to go sideways if nobody's coordinating.
body.1: Most brokers don't set up to coordinate ocean shipping. They quote you a price, take a deposit, then hand you a phone number for a port agent who has never heard of you. We don't.

whyDifferent.punchline: Your coordinator owns the entire chain — from California pickup to your driveway in Honolulu, Hilo, Kahului, or Lihue. One phone number, one accountable point of contact.

route.title: Six legs. One coordinator. Locked price.

1.title: Mainland pickup
1.detail: Carrier dispatched to your California address. Driver completes 360° walkaround photos and signed BOL. You're CC'd on the dispatch confirmation.

2.title: Port transit
2.detail: Vehicle delivered to either Long Beach or Oakland port (whichever optimizes total cost + timeline). We choose; you don't manage.

3.title: Port booking + ocean carrier
3.detail: Booking confirmed with the ocean carrier (Matson or Pasha typically). Sail date set. You get the vessel name, sail date, and ETA in your portal.

4.title: Ocean voyage
4.detail: 5–10 days port-to-port to Honolulu. Vehicle is secured below deck (RoRo) or in containerized service depending on booking type.

5.title: Honolulu port retrieval
5.detail: Our island agent receives the vehicle. Inter-island barge booked here if your destination is Maui, Big Island, or Kauai (adds 2–4 days).

6.title: Final delivery
6.detail: Vehicle delivered to your Hawaii address. Walkaround photos at delivery. BOL signed. You and your coordinator both have the documentation.

subDestinations.title: All four major islands. Each with its own routing.
subDestinations.description: Honolulu is the primary mainland-Hawaii port — every shipment passes through it. Outer-island deliveries add an inter-island barge leg with its own timeline.

specialty.title: Hawaii has specific rules. We've seen them all.
specialty.description: Ocean transit + Hawaii Department of Agriculture inspections add constraints that don't exist on mainland routes. Here's what we navigate for the most common situations.

ev.title: Electric vehicles (EVs)
ev.detail: IMDG Class 9 hazmat regulations apply for lithium-ion batteries during ocean transit. Carriers cap charge state at 30–50% at port. We confirm specifics at booking. No extra fee from us for compliance handling.

classic.title: Classic / collector cars
classic.summary: Enclosed-trailer + higher insurance valuation strongly recommended.
classic.detail: For vehicles over $75K or with custom paint, wraps, or unrestored bodywork. Inspection at pickup is more thorough — we document condition with extra photo coverage. Pickup ports are the same; carrier mix differs.

motorcycle.title: Motorcycles
motorcycle.summary: Crated container or strapped trailer — typically lower cost than cars.
motorcycle.detail: Ocean carriers require fuel drained below 1/4 tank. Battery state checked at pickup. Both single bikes and multi-bike shipments handled. Helmet and gear up to ~30 lbs can ride along inside.

oversized.title: Oversized / RVs
oversized.detail: Vehicles exceeding standard carrier height (~7') or length ship on flatbed or specialized trailer. Surcharge scales with dimensions. Quoted separately — your coordinator will need exact measurements.

personal.title: Personal items in vehicle
personal.summary: Up to ~100 lbs in trunk. Strict exclusions.
personal.detail: NO firearms, ammunition, alcohol, hazmat, or live plants (Hawaii ag restrictions are strict). Items must be secured against shifting. Damage to personal items isn't covered — load anything you do bring at your own risk.

ag.title: Agricultural quarantine prep
ag.summary: Required by Hawaii Department of Ag. We coordinate.
ag.detail: Hawaii inspects incoming vehicles for soil and plant material. Undercarriage must be cleaned thoroughly before pickup. Failure to pass = vehicle held at port (your cost). We send prep instructions at booking.

specialty.footnote: Don't see your situation? Mention it in the quote-request notes field. Your coordinator will confirm handling and any additional cost before booking.

tiers.title: Three tiers. Same locked-price guarantee on all three.
tiers.description: Pick the urgency level that fits your timeline. Live pricing comes through the quote tool — pulled from our verified carrier network at the moment you book.

standby.description: Best value. Vehicle catches the next available sailing within ~2 weeks of pickup.

priority.description: Most common for PCS and planned relocations. Specific sail booked at quote time.

expedited.description: When the timeline is tight. We push to the soonest viable sail and accept the premium.

tiers.footnote: Pricing varies by season (peak May–Sept), vehicle type, and destination island. Get a quote for your specific shipment.

triplePromise.eyebrow: The Triple Promise applies the same way
triplePromise.title: Across six legs and an ocean.

0.q: How long does shipping a car from California to Hawaii take?
0.a: Plan on 10–18 days door-to-door in most cases. The ocean leg is typically 5–10 days port-to-port (Long Beach or Oakland to Honolulu), plus 2–5 days for mainland pickup and inland trucking on each end. Outer islands (Maui, Kauai, Big Island) add another 2–4 days for inter-island barge.

1.q: Which port do you use — Long Beach or Oakland?
1.a: Both are options. Long Beach has more sailings per week and slightly lower base rates from Southern California pickups. Oakland is faster for Bay Area and Northern California pickups. We pick whichever lands a better total cost + timeline for your specific origin. The choice is invisible to you in the quote.

2.q: Do I have to drop my car at the port myself?
2.a: No. Standard service is door-to-door: we send a carrier to your California pickup address, transport to the port, manage the ocean booking and dock paperwork, and receive the vehicle on the Hawaii side for final delivery to your address. You can also choose port-to-port for a lower price if you want to handle the inland legs yourself.

3.q: What about my car insurance during the ocean voyage?
3.a: Carrier cargo coverage stays in effect for the full transit. Our Damage Promise (broker bond + $500K contingent cargo) backstops the ocean carrier the same way it backstops mainland carriers. You file one claim with us — we handle all the carrier-side complexity.

4.q: Can I leave personal items in my car for the ocean shipment?
4.a: Up to 100 lbs of personal items in the trunk is generally fine. NO firearms, ammunition, alcohol, hazmat, or live plants (Hawaii agricultural restrictions are strict). Items shifting during transit aren't covered for damage — secure anything you do load. When in doubt, your coordinator will flag specific items before booking.

5.q: Do you ship to all four major islands?
5.a: Yes — Oahu (Honolulu), Maui (Kahului), Big Island (Hilo or Kona), and Kauai (Lihue). Honolulu is the primary mainland-to-Hawaii port, with inter-island barge to outer islands as the second leg. Inter-island timing is typically 2–4 additional days.

6.q: How does pricing work for military PCS shipments?
6.a: Active-duty military with PCS orders qualify for our military rate (typically 5–10% below standard). We work with all branches — Army, Navy, Air Force, Marines, Coast Guard. Bring your orders to booking; your coordinator applies the discount automatically. POV shipment reimbursement processes through your TMO — we provide the documentation needed.

finalCta.title: See your locked Hawaii price in 60 seconds.
finalCta.description: Pickup ZIP, destination island, vehicle type. We'll show three tiered prices. Pick one, lock it, and your coordinator takes it from there.

metadata.title: Ship a car California to Alaska — PCS, oil/gas, retiree relocations
metadata.description: California-to-Alaska vehicle transport via overland (Alaska Highway) or ocean (Tacoma to Anchorage). Locked-price quotes. Trusted by military PCS to JBER, Eielson, and Fort Wainwright.

hero.title: Ship a car to Alaska. Two routes. One coordinator.
hero.description: Overland via the Alaska Highway or ocean via Tacoma. Optimized for season, destination, and timeline. Locked price either way.

whyDifferent.title: Alaska is two corridors pretending to be one.

body.0: Most California-to-Alaska shipping is for one of three customers: military PCS families heading to JBER, Eielson, or Fort Wainwright; oil and gas workers rotating through the North Slope; retirees moving permanently. Each group has different timeline tolerance, route preferences, and vehicle types — and the right answer for one isn't always the right answer for another.
body.1: Then there's the seasonality. Winter on the Alaska Highway means weather closures, frost heaves, and trucking premiums. Ocean from Tacoma stays reliable year-round. Summer flips the math — overland gets faster and cheaper for interior destinations.

whyDifferent.punchline: Your coordinator picks the route based on your specific origin, destination, season, and timeline. You see one locked price for whichever route they recommend.

routes.title: Ocean vs. overland — same coordinator, different logistics.

points.1: Faster for interior destinations in summer

subDestinations.title: Four destination regions. Different routing for each.
subDestinations.description: Anchorage and Mat-Su use ocean shipping by default. Fairbanks can shift between routes seasonally. Southeast Alaska routes through the Marine Highway System with longer timelines.

specialty.title: Alaska customers ship different vehicles than mainland customers.
specialty.description: Lifted trucks, oversized SUVs, ATVs paired with the main vehicle — common asks here, not so much elsewhere. Here's what we navigate for the situations that come up.

lifted.title: Lifted trucks
lifted.summary: Yes — common for AK customers. Surcharge scales with height.
lifted.detail: Lifts under ~6" typically fit standard carriers. Lifts of 6–10" require specialized carriers and a height surcharge. Lifts over 10" or with oversized tires may need flatbed transport. Confirmed at quote time based on actual specs.

oversizedSuv.title: Oversized SUVs / dual-axle
oversizedSuv.summary: Standard for the AK customer base. Premium scales with dimensions.
oversizedSuv.detail: Excursions, Suburbans, dually trucks all routine. Vehicles exceeding standard carrier slot (length, weight, or height) shift into the oversized pricing tier. Dimensions confirmed at booking; price stays locked once confirmed.

atv.title: ATVs / snowmobiles paired
atv.summary: Pair with main vehicle on the same dispatch — saves a full second booking.
atv.detail: Common for AK customers shipping their primary vehicle plus an ATV or snowmobile. Both can ride on the same carrier with the snowmobile/ATV typically secured in a trailer or strapped to a flatbed alongside the main vehicle. Saves ~30–40% vs separate dispatches.

rv.title: RVs / motorhomes
rv.summary: Specialized carrier network. Quote requires VIN + class + dimensions.
rv.detail: Class A, B, and C motorhomes ship through a separate carrier pool from passenger vehicles. Tow-behind RVs (5th wheels, travel trailers) ship more like cars. Either way, your coordinator confirms route + carrier match before quoting.

rooftop.title: Vehicles with rooftop accessories
rooftop.summary: Disclose at booking. Some affect carrier slot height.
rooftop.detail: Cargo boxes, ski racks, light bars, and roof tents add height. A few inches usually fits standard slots; aggressive setups may require specialized carriers. Always disclose at quote — we'd rather adjust pricing up front than surprise you at pickup.

coldPrep.title: Cold-weather prep
coldPrep.summary: Required for any winter transit. Sent at booking.
coldPrep.detail: Drain or add antifreeze to washer fluid (it ruptures when frozen). Fuel below 1/2 tank (carrier requirement). Battery in good charge state — cold storage can drain weak batteries. Tire pressure adjusted for the destination climate. We send a checklist at booking.

specialty.footnote: Don't see your situation? Mention it in the quote-request notes field. Your coordinator will confirm handling and any additional cost before booking.

tiers.title: Three tiers. Same locked-price guarantee on all three.
tiers.description: Pick the urgency level that fits your timeline. Live pricing comes through the quote tool — pulled from our verified carrier network at the moment you book.

standby.description: Best value. Catches the next available carrier or sailing within ~10 days of pickup.

priority.description: Most common for PCS and planned relocations. Specific dispatch scheduled at quote time.

expedited.description: When timing trumps price. We get on the soonest viable carrier or sailing.

tiers.footnote: Pricing varies by season (winter ocean preferred), vehicle size, and destination. Active-duty military PCS rates apply automatically with orders.

triplePromise.eyebrow: The Triple Promise applies the same way
triplePromise.title: Across 3,500 miles or an ocean voyage.

0.q: How long does shipping a car from California to Alaska take?
0.a: Two route options. Ocean route (Tacoma → Anchorage) runs about 8–14 days door-to-door including the inland legs. Overland route via the Alaska Highway runs 7–12 days door-to-door. Winter delays are real (especially Nov–Mar) and we pad timelines accordingly. Your coordinator will recommend the right route based on season + final destination.

1.q: Ocean vs. overland — which should I pick?
1.a: Ocean is the default for Anchorage and Mat-Su Valley destinations. Predictable timing, no weather closures, lower carrier wear-and-tear on the vehicle. Overland can be faster in summer for Fairbanks and interior destinations, but the Alaska Highway has weather sensitivity (snowstorms, road closures, frost heaves). For most customers most of the year, ocean is the better choice. We'll quote both and recommend based on your specific situation.

2.q: Do you ship to military bases — JBER, Eielson, Fort Wainwright?
2.a: Yes. Military bases are a primary use case for this corridor. Joint Base Elmendorf-Richardson (JBER) in Anchorage, Eielson AFB and Fort Wainwright near Fairbanks, Coast Guard Base Kodiak — we ship to all of them regularly. Bring your PCS orders to booking and your coordinator applies the military rate (typically 5–10% below standard).

3.q: What about winter? Is the road closed?
3.a: The Alaska Highway is technically open year-round, but winter conditions (Oct–Apr) make trucking risky and slow. We strongly recommend ocean shipping for any winter shipment to avoid weather delays, frost heaves, and the occasional avalanche closure. Ocean carriers run reliably year-round from Tacoma. Pricing is similar either way — winter premium is small.

4.q: Can I leave personal items in the vehicle?
4.a: Up to 100 lbs in the trunk is generally fine. Same restrictions as most corridors: no firearms, ammunition, alcohol, or hazmat. Cold-weather note: liquids that can freeze (water bottles, washer fluid, canned beverages) can rupture in unheated transit. Drain or remove before pickup.

5.q: What about my car insurance during overland or ocean transit?
5.a: Carrier cargo coverage stays in effect for the full transit either route. Our Damage Promise (broker bond + $500K contingent cargo) backstops the carrier's coverage gap. Single claim filed with us — we coordinate the carrier-side resolution.

6.q: Do you handle oversized or lifted vehicles?
6.a: Yes — common in Alaska due to the truck-heavy customer base. Lifted trucks, oversized SUVs, and trucks with rooftop accessories ship regularly. Pricing premium scales with size: lifted vehicles add $150–$400 over standard, oversized may require enclosed trailer upgrade. Your coordinator confirms exact pricing at quote time.

finalCta.title: See your locked Alaska price in 60 seconds.
finalCta.description: Pickup ZIP, destination, vehicle type. We'll quote both routes and recommend the right one. Pick a tier, lock the price, and your coordinator takes it from there.
